content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 12, 2019

Celia Brewer
City Attorney
Office of the City Attorney
1200 Carlsbad Village Drive
Carlsbad, CA 92008

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-019

Dear Ms. Brewer:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Carlsbad Councilmember Barbara Hamilton regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

1. May Councilmember Hamilton participate in decisions involving the Buena Vista Lagoon Enhancement Project ("Project") where she has a real property interest located within 500 feet of the lagoon's property line?

2. May she serve on the Buena Vista Lagoon Joint Powers Committee ("JPC")?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-019
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

1. Yes. You have presented clear evidence that the lagoon enhancement decisions will not
have any measurable impact on her property.

2. The Act does not prohibit her from serving on the JPC. The Act does prohibit her
participation in decisions where it will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect
on her property, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. Please request additional
advice, as needed, for any JPC decisions that raise this issue.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The Buena Vista Lagoon is a freshwater coastal lagoon located along Carlsbad's northern
city limit. The lagoon encompasses approximately 220 acres of wetland habitat, extending to the
coastline from just east of Interstate 5. It serves as a wetland habitat and a geographic border
between Carlsbad and Oceanside. It is not open to the public for recreational use. According to the
Project Fact Sheet, the lagoon is in a state of biological decline related to sedimentation and water
quality issues. These conditions have diminished the lagoon's value to fish and wildlife as well as
human use.² Over the past decade numerous federal, state, and local agencies and organizations
have attempted to reach consensus on how to reverse this decline through a large-scale
enhancement effort. The cities of Oceanside and Carlsbad also have included lagoon preservation in
their subarea plans under the San Diego County Multiple Habitat Conservation Plan.

The Buena Vista Lagoon JPC is a committee made up of two city council members and a
resident of Carlsbad, along with representatives from the cities of Vista and Oceanside. The goal of
the committee is to maintain a sustainable Buena Vista Lagoon and Watershed ecosystem through
active planning and program management.

The San Diego Association of Governments ("SANDAG") is the lead agency for the
environmental review of the project. SANDAG completed an Environmental Impact Report
("EIR"), which analyzed a variety of alternatives, and provided a recommendation on how best to
achieve the lagoon's enhancement. Four project alternatives were evaluated: three enhancement
alternatives (Freshwater, Saltwater, and a Saltwater/Freshwater Hybrid) and a "No Project"
alternative. SANDAG engaged federal and state agencies, the cities of Carlsbad and Oceanside,
stakeholders, and the public in considering alternatives through the EIR process. The Final EIR
identities, and the City of Carlsbad supports, the Saltwater Alternative as the proposed project due
to its potential to reverse the ecological decline of the lagoon while reducing flood risks
and improving water quality. The salt water alternative will also provide enhanced habitat for
endangered species and reduce the presence of mosquitos. Future enhancements to the Buena Vista
Lagoon could also serve as mitigation for potential impacts from future Interstate 5 and north
county rail improvements pursuant to the North Coast Corridor Public Works Plan. Currently, the
Project is stayed until May of 2019 for all parties to discuss further alternatives and solutions.

Councilmember Hamilton has a residence within 500 feet of a portion of the lagoon's
property line. The lagoon sits on the east and west side of I-5, and her property is closest to the

² https://www.sandag.org/uploads/publicationid/publicationid_1751_15914.pdf, viewed 3/6/2019.

File No. A-19-019
Page No. 3

eastern portion of the lagoon. However, her property and the lagoon are separated by a steep bluff
and several vegetated lots as shown in the map image provided with the request. The vegetated lots
are separated from the lagoon property by Jefferson Street, a major roadway. Due to the topography
of the area, Councilmember Hamilton has only a small view of the lagoon from a corner of the
northwest side of her property. Her property does not have direct access to the lagoon. Additionally,
her property interest is more than 500 feet from Interstate 5 and the north county railroad. She has
more than a $2,000 direct investment in her real property.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The financial interest
implicated by these facts is the Councilmember's real property interest. (Section 87103(b).)

Foreseeability and Materiality

The standard for foreseeability varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly
involved in the decision at issue. A financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable where the
official's financial interest is explicitly involved as a named party in, or subject of, the decision.³
(Regulation 18701(a).) For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, the
financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation
18701(b).)

Decisions to enhance or not enhance the lagoon relate to issues of the lagoon's water quality
and wetlands restoration. These enhancements do not appear to provide improved services that
benefit or are a detriment to the Councilmember's parcel, and do not appear to affect her property
disproportionate to those received by other properties in near the lagoon. (Regulation
18702.2(a)(6).) Therefore, her residence is not explicitly involved in the enhancement decisions,
and the latter foreseeability standard applies.

³ A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial, o revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, or if the
decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). Where a decision involves
improvements to facilities, such as water, sewer or storm drainage, the parcel is deemed explicitly involved if the
improved services provide a benefit or detriment to the parcel disproportionate to other properties receiving the service.
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(6).) See also, footnote 4, below.

File No. A-19-019
Page No. 4

Real Property

Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standard for real property interests, which varies depending on the proximity of the decision's impacts to the property interest.⁴ Where a decision involves property located 500 feet or less from the property line of the parcel, there is a presumption that the decision will have a material financial effect on the official's property unless clear and convincing evidence is presented that the decision will not have any measurable impact on the official's property. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(7).) In this matter, the Councilmember's property is separated from the lagoon by three geographical barriers: a steep bluff, several vegetated lots, and a major roadway. She does not have a direct access to the lagoon, and it is only partially visible from the corner of her parcel. The enhancements, with their focus on restoring the wetland habitat in an area not open to the public, do not appear to be the type of enhancements that impact property values. Given the geographic separation and the type of enhancements involved, you have provided clear and convincing evidence that these decisions will not have any measurable impact on her property. Therefore, the Councilmember is not prohibited from participating in the Project decisions.

As to serving on the JPC which seeks to maintain a sustainable lagoon and watershed ecosystem through planning and program management, the Act does not prohibit her service on this committee. However, her real property financial interest may prohibit her participation in JPC decisions where it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on her residential property, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Regulations 18702.2 and 18703.) Please contact our office for additional advice if a JPC decision may involve her financial interest in her residence.


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

⁴ Regulation 18702.2 was amended by the Commission on January 17, 2019 and is effective at the end of March, 2019. We apply the amended version of this regulation for this analysis.
